post: ð§  Can EEG Have Its Own "ChatGPT" Moment? (EEGPT?) When ChatGPT was released, it first learned the general structure of language from lots of text. Later, it could be adapted to writing, coding, translation, summarization, and many other tasks. The same idea is now reaching neuroscience and researchers are beginning to build foundation models that first learn the "language" of EEG from massive datasets. One example is EEGPT (NeurIPS 2024). It is pretrained using self-supervised learning on large collections of EEG recordings. Its goal is to learn general representations of brain activity that can later be adapted to many downstream tasks. E

post: One clinical observation from our EEG-informed workflow After several posts about the engineering logic of our project, I would like to show how this approach looked in one case selected from a series of practical clinical observations. This was not the only practical observation. I selected this particular case because its EEG spectral features were especially clear and useful for illustrating the workflow of the method. The workflow included: EEG recording; mathematical processing; comparison of selected electrode pairs; spectral analysis; calculation of an individual stimulation signal; stimulation course; follow-up assessment. post: You've heard of mobile EEG. Thanks to Morph Labs, we might have to coin "Nomadic EEG." In the last year, we've collected data and built our company across 2 cities and 11 different offices. A few weeks in one place, then pack up and move to the next. No permanent lab, no fixed setup, no settling in. Just show up, collect, learn, and do it again somewhere new. We once kept using an office after the group we'd borrowed it from moved out, running for a week in an empty room with one chair, one table, and no soap in the bathrooms, until the owners sent people to check the space and kicked us out. But every move pushed the work a little further, a
item_title: Neurolife launch brings non invasive technology aimed at restoring hand and arm movement a

post: Excited to share our most recent paper examining how low-frequency rTMS to the right dorsolateral prefrontal cortex may exert antidepressant effects in treatment-resistant depression. Using concurrent TMS-fMRI, we were able to observe the acute brain-network effects of stimulation in real-time. In this secondary analysis of clinical trial data, 38 participants with treatment-resistant depression underwent 1 Hz TMS to the right DLPFC during fMRI, followed by a 4-week treatment course using the same protocol. We focused on the salience, control, and default mode networks — the so-called “triple networks” — given their central role in models of
item_title: Large-scale brain networks and psychopathology: a unifying triple network model

post: What if cognition and consciousness emerge not from individual brain states, but from the way the brain transitions between them? A fascinating new Trends in Neurosciences review explores EEG microstates—brief, recurring patterns of brain activity that may represent fundamental units of large-scale neural dynamics. The paper connects these transient states to cognition, consciousness, schizophrenia, Alzheimer's disease, sleep, anesthesia, and disorders of consciousness, while also challenging several long-standing assumptions in the field. This review also brought me back to questions I explored years ago regarding neuromodulation and cortica

post: Neural networks were inspired by the human brain. So a question kept bothering me: If they are inspired by the brain, shouldn’t there be similarities in how both systems internally represent information? I’ve been working on a project comparing emotional representations inside LLMs and human brain fMRI data- not just outputs, but the actual geometry of how emotions are organised internally in a multi dimensional space. Some interesting findings: • Emotional ambiguity emerges from competition between emotional prototypes • Both brains and LLMs show similar local geometric behaviour • Emotion in LLMs exists in surprisingly small low-rank subspa
item_title: When brains and llms start thinking similarly about emotion de3615082df2

post: Why EEG can be useful before brain stimulation An electroencephalogram is not a “photograph of thought”. It does not automatically diagnose a patient, and it does not directly tell us how to treat a specific condition. But EEG can provide something important for brain-stimulation research: individual data about electrical brain activity over time. In our project, we use EEG not as a standalone diagnostic tool, but as a source of measurable signal data: - frequency structure; - spectral power; - differences between electrode positions; - asymmetries between functionally related areas; - changes before and after stimulation. This is important b

post: The number of times I’ve had the “brain scan” conversation in the office… A patient (and I don’t blame them) wants to see something. They want an image. A marker. Proof that what they’re feeling has a physical correlate. And every time, I have to explain the same frustrating truth: for most psychiatric conditions, the resolution just isn’t there yet. You can spend thousands on a snazzy fMRI and, more often than not, I’ll still be left saying some version of, “Yep… there’s a brain in there.” But I almost always end that conversation the same way: “It’s so close I can taste it.” Today, I got a taste. post: One fun thing about this paper is that they used a Karpathy-style AutoResearch loop to find better configurations of the optimizer. They lowered their WER by \~10% compared to an Optuna baseline this way. The agents are not quite autonomously implementing ML yet (they tried and it crashed and burned on SLURM configs, respect), but you can see the embers of it. A senior Anthropic engineer just dropped an 11-page PDF on "Loop Engineering" for agentic systems. The shift: you stop prompting the agent. You build the system that prompts it instead. post: Stress is now wearable. For the first time, you can track your cortisol the way you track your steps. Adaptyx Biosciences just unveiled it. The Menlo Park-based Stanford spinout shared what they believe is the first continuous, multi-day measurement of free cortisol in humans using a wearable sensor. The device is a minimally-invasive patch with a dermal probe that reads biomarkers in your interstitial fluid using programmable molecular switches. Translation: instead of one snapshot from a blood draw, you get a continuous stream of your stress hormone, in real time. The company raised $14M in seed financing in October 2025, bringing total fun
item_title: A harness for every task dynamic workflows in claude code

post: I connected Function Health and HealthKit to Anthropic's Claude through MCPs last month. Asked a question that spanned both datasets. Lab trends against real-time vitals. Got a useful answer in seconds. Then I started a different health conversation. Claude has memory. It can reference past conversations. You can build Projects with reference files. I've done all of this. But for a question requiring real depth, I still had to re-establish foundational information: full lab history, genetic predispositions, complete supplement stack with timelines. The structured context that determines whether an answer is generic or precise. MCPs give live
item_title: Yann lecun discusses a new approach to ai in browns lemley family leadership lecture serie

post: Everyone in my Harvard Business School classroom is debating who's winning the AI model wars. Claude vs. ChatGPT vs. Gemini. Which lab is ahead. Which model is best. I think that's the wrong question — and history tells us why. In my latest essay on Learn with Professor KL, I go back to 1926 — when the auto industry looked exactly like this. Hundreds of firms. Competing designs. Weekly shifts in who seemed to be "winning." We know how that story ended. But nobody living through it could see the shakeout coming. Two scholars changed how I think about moments like this: James Utterback (MIT) and Steven Klepper (CMU). Their frameworks — dominant
item_title: From ferment to dominant design reading

post: What does deterministic AI mean in a regulated environment? It does not mean making a probabilistic model deterministic. It means building systems where outputs are controlled for intended use. In practice, that comes down to discipline around data, execution, and verification. The system operates on a governed and consistent view of data, not on shifting or loosely interpreted sources. Requests are resolved through predefined, constrained pathways rather than open-ended interpretation. Execution is structured, repeatable, and bounded. Every result is accompanied by sufficient context to understand how it was produced—what data was used, how
item_title: used, how it was processed, and whether it met defined acceptance criteria.

post: Some of the most challenging cases I see are patients carrying two diagnoses at once. New real-world data from BrainsWay out today in Psychiatric Times looks at Deep TMS in 462 patients living with both PTSD and major depression, the combination that tends to bring more severe symptoms, higher suicide risk, and a poorer response to standard treatment. Across 11 sites, 83.5% saw a meaningful reduction in PTSD symptoms and two thirds saw the same in their depression. This one is personal for me. post: Stanford Psychiatry's Umair Hassan has received a grant from the National Institute of Mental Health ( NIH Innovates) titled "Sleep theta burst stimulation for improved prefrontal neuromodulation in depression." "We will investigate whether delivering intermittent theta burst stimulation to the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex during non-rapid eye movement sleep, particularly slow-wave sleep, can enhance prefrontal plasticity in depression by aligning with naturally occurring periods of heightened neural plasticity," says Dr. Hassan. "By leveraging the brain's intrinsic plasticity mechanisms during sleep, this innovative approach holds promise

post: ð§   There’s a moment in elite performance when conscious control gets quieter. What does that look like in the data? Watching Jonathan Wolpaw's lecture at the BCI & Neurotechnology Spring School 2026 organized by g.tec medical engineering GmbH - NEVER STOP RECORDING, something clicked. A skill isn't stored anywhere. It's maintained by distributed networks of neurons and synapses that constantly adjust to keep the outcome stable. Wolpaw calls them hexers, from the ancient Greek hexus: not an act, but a disposition, a state of possession. Bernstein's work feels connected to this idea. He filmed an expert hitting a chisel with a hammer. Every

post: ð§  Before taking neuroscience to the field, you need to know where to look. EEG studies of motor performance sit on a spectrum between signal quality and ecological validity. At one end: stationary lab EEG with maximal signal quality and low ecological validity. In the middle: lab-grade wireless EEG still in the lab with repeatable and constrained sport movements. At the other end: fully ecological field recordings with maximal behavioral realism and maximal noise. Piskin et al. (2025) is a middle-of-the-spectrum study. They used a 65-channel mobile EEG system (actiCap, Brain Products GmbH) during a kicking task performed in a controlled la
item_title: Adhd autism depression diagnoses.html?unlocked article code=1.hla.chvg.rcxtwwvgxfgj&smid=n

post: Most people think the psychedelic experience is what heals. They're wrong. It's what happens in the days and weeks AFTER that determines everything. In 2023, Gül Dölen's lab at Johns Hopkins made a discovery that should reshape how every practitioner thinks about their work: MDMA, psilocybin, LSD, ketamine, and ibogaine all reopen the social reward learning critical period, those rare developmental windows when we learn how to bond, trust, and adapt. Our brains were once thought to close these windows by age 26. Turns out, psychedelics hand us the key to unlock them again. Here's what makes this research so compelling: Different compounds hit
